title: `podman login` writes no content into /tmp/
body:
/kind bug

**Description**
While _Podman_ correctly detects _systemd_ and uses the default `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR`, some parts of `podman login` seems to have a small bug.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
```
sudo -u podman podman --log-level debug login
```
<details>

```
INFO[0000] podman filtering at log level debug
DEBU[0000] Called login.PersistentPreRunE(podman --log-level debug login)
DEBU[0000] Using conmon: "/usr/local/bin/conmon"
```

</details>

As you can see, the directory is empty. The correct content is in `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR`
```
ls -al /tmp/containers-user-1001/containers/
```
<details>

```
total 8
drwx------ 2 podman podman 4096 Jun 25 11:47 .
drwx------ 3 podman podman 4096 Jun 25 11:47 ..
```
</details>

```
sudo -u podman printenv
```
As expected, `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` hasn't been set without `sudo --login`
<details>

```
LANG=en_US.UTF-8
TERM=xterm-256color
PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
MAIL=/var/mail/podman
LOGNAME=podman
USER=podman
HOME=/home/podman
SHELL=/usr/sbin/nologin
SUDO_COMMAND=/usr/bin/printenv
SUDO_USER=root
SUDO_UID=0
SUDO_GID=0
```
</details>

```
machinectl shell podman@.host /usr/bin/printenv
```
<details>

```
LANG=en_US.UTF-8
PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin
HOME=/home/podman
LOGNAME=podman
USER=podman
INVOCATION_ID=ad6acc5d464d4c539ff483e0ce88db73
TERM=xterm-256color
MAIL=/var/mail/podman
XDG_SESSION_ID=23
XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/1001
DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS=unix:path=/run/user/1001/bus
XDG_SESSION_TYPE=tty
XDG_SESSION_CLASS=user
```
</details>

When using `machinectl` (or `sudo --login`), `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` gets set and no directory is created in `tmp`
```
rm -rf /tmp/containers-user-1001/
machinectl shell podman@.host /usr/local/bin/podman --log-level debug login
```

**Describe the results you received:**
Added to the steps above for better assignment.

**Describe the results you expected:**
Since the default `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` gets used, there shouldn't be an empty directory-structure in `/tmp/`

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
I'm aware that the variable `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` is mandatory but seems like something changed in _Podman_ a few versions ago. It seems like _Podman_ automatically chooses the default `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` when it can communicate with _systemd_. 

It seems like at least `podman login` doesn't has been updated. It creates the directories in `/tmp/`, but without content. The credentials are written at the right place: below `/run/user/1001/`.

_systemd_'s `user-linger` has been activated of course. I am aware that you normally need `machinectl` (suggested by _systemd_-community) or `sudo --login` to get a login environment (not possible if it's a system user), _Podman_ seems to auto-detect `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` now.


**Output of `podman version`:**

```
Version:      3.3.0-dev
API Version:  3.3.0-dev
Go Version:   go1.16.5
Git Commit:   ed511d27d7d2198624dcda41b9e06e9e582e5c20-dirty
Built:        Tue Jun 22 16:27:53 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

FrankenDebian: Debian Bullseye RC1 on Ubuntu Mainline Kernel 5.13.0-051300rc6-generic on VirtualBox

comments.body: I really do not understand what the bug is,   Are you saying the podman is creating /tmp/containers-user-1001/containers/ when even when XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is defined?

No, but since some builds (I cannot tell the exact commit), _podman_ doesn't complain about a missing `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` anymore. 

If `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` isn't set, _podman_ uses the default location (`/run/user/1001/`) and writes its stuff into. But in this case, `/tmp/containers-user-1001/containers/` gets created without any content and doesn't seem to be used at all.

Ok, I would guess that there is different code handling different parts of the system, and one looks to see if a writable /run/user/* exists if XDG_RUNTIME_DIR does not exist and another piece just goes write to /tmp/containers-user-1001/containers/
Can you do a podman info on the container and which paths are in there?  I think containers/storage is creating the content in /tmp.

I have to revert fix  containers/storage#952

Was this happening on a non systemd system?

> I have to revert fix [containers/storage#952](https://github.com/containers/storage/pull/952)

I mean, _understanding_ both this and https://github.com/containers/storage/pull/568 is the fulcrum. It’s not yet obvious to me that the revert is definite.

I’ve been fairly vocal in #568 about not caring about an unnamed vaguely-specified environment that motivated that PR. Maybe it doesn’t even exist any more. Maybe this systemd detection code is buggy. Maybe the two issues are an irreconcilable difference in fairly rare environments; maybe it’s the case and we still have a clear reason to prioritize one or the other.

Whatever happens, let’s please make sure we have a written record of _why_ we have decided for one design or another. In one more year or a few more, it’s pretty likely that these tradeoffs will again be questioned by whatever unfortunate soul is tasked with maintaining this fallback logic; so let’s preserve our hard-earned knowledge instead of rediscovering the tradeoffs and design constraints every year, and starting from the questionable scratch over and over.

> Was this happening on a non systemd system?

Assuming this is systemd as mentioned above, please capture, from the same environment that starts Podman:
- Full metadata of `/proc/1/comm` , including size, ownership, permissions, SELinux context, any other security-relevant attributes (AppArmor? something else?)
- Exact contents of `/proc/1/comm`
- Maybe `strace` of Podman’s system calls related to that file could help.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@Luap99 does your change to unset XDG_RUNTIME_DIR for root, fix this issue?
Do we even know what the issue _is_, yet?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we have no input, I am closing.  Reopen if you still want this worked on, and can provide more data.
